question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers: nan

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers:
Objective: The aim of this study was to describe the malignancy rates
in childhood and adult-onset systemic lupus erythematous (SLE) by
proportional meta-analysis.

Studies were included if participants had an established diagnosis of SLE by clinical evaluation and classification criteria,3,4 regardless of age, sex, or
exposure to steroids or immunosuppressive or biologic treatment. This review included all longitudinal studies with malignancy reports or a proper score for the SLICC/American College of Rheumatology Damage Index (SDI). The SDI scores 1 point for any biopsy-proven malignancy from each site, excluding dysplasia, after 6 months of SLE onset.18 Those longitudinal studies were mainly cohort studies with a minimum 6 months' followup and cancer registry reports. We excluded reviews and crosssectional and case-control studies. The primary outcome was any type of malignancy, regardless of topography.

after 6 months of SLE onset.18 Those longitudinal studies
were mainly cohort studies with a minimum 6 months' followup

any type of malignancy, regardless of topography.

answers:
We excluded reviews and crosssectional
and case-control studies.

Studies with incomplete data about the outcome were excluded
from the review.

answers: There was no language restriction

answers: reference lists of the identified relevant studies were scrutinized for additional citations

question: Were the terms and structure of the search strategy likely to retrieve as many eligible studies as possible?
answers:
APPENDIX 1. Search strategy
(systemic lupus erythematosus OR Lupus Erythematosus Disseminatus OR Libman-Sacks Disease OR Libman Sacks Disease) AND (Neoplasms
OR Neoplasm OR Tumors OR Tumor OR Cancer OR Cancers OR Benign Neoplasms OR Benign Neoplasm OR malignancy OR malignancies
OR Hematological Neoplasms OR Hematological Neoplasm OR Hematologic Neoplasm OR Hematological Malignancies OR Hematological
Malignancy OR Hematologic Malignancy OR Hematologic Malignancies OR Hematopoietic Neoplasms OR Hematopoietic Malignancies OR
Hematopoietic Malignancy OR Hematopoietic Neoplasm OR blood cancers OR liquid tumors OR solid tumors OR solid tumor OR solid neoplasm
OR solid neoplasms OR solid cancers OR solid cancer OR organ tumors OR organ tumor) AND (incidence[MeSH:noexp] OR mortality
[MeSH Terms] OR follow up studies[MeSH:noexp] OR prognos*[TextWord] OR prognosis OR prognostic OR prognostics OR predicts OR risk
factor OR risk factors OR predict*[TextWord] OR course*[TextWord]) OR prognosis*[Title/Abstract] OR (first[Title/Abstract] AND episode
[Title/Abstract]) OR cohort[Title/Abstract]) OR (controlled trial$*) OR (randomised controlled trial$*)

question: Were search strategy restrictions based on date, publication format, or language appropriate?
answers: There was no language restriction

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in selection of studies?
answers: Two of the authors reviewed the articles independently, to extract the data.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in data collection?
answers: Two of the authors reviewed the articles independently, to extract the data.

question: Were sufficient study characteristics considered for both review authors and readers to be able to interpret the results?
answers:
Population characteristics by country, number of participants,
and follow-up time are presented in the Table 1. Among the included
studies, 23 were from Western countries (United States,
Canada, Latin America, and Europe),5,9,15,24-42 whereas 5 studies
were from eastern countries (Hong Kong and Korea),43-47 and
2 studies were from Middle East (Saudi Arabia and Israel).48,49
Sample size in each of the studies ranged from 47 to 30,478 subjects,
with females being the majority.27,46 Only 2 studies reported
specific types of malignancy, breast and lung cancer, respectively9,32;
the remaining reported overall malignancy rates (Table 1).

TABLE 1. Population Characteristics of Included Cohorts

question: Were all relevant study results collected for use in the synthesis?
answers: TABLE 1. Population Characteristics of Included Cohorts

question: Was risk of bias (or methodological quality) formally assessed using appropriate criteria?
answers: We evaluated the risk of bias of the selected articles using a modified version of the Ottawa-Newcastle instrument.19 It includes confidence in assessment of exposure and outcome and adjusted analysis for differences between groups in prognostic characteristics or missing data.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in risk of bias assessment?
answers: nan

question: Did the synthesis include all studies that it should?
answers: Results of 30 articles with a total of 96,578 subjects were included in the meta- analysis.

question: Were all pre-defined analyses reported or departures explained?
answers: nan

question: Was the synthesis appropriate given the nature and similarity in the research questions, study designs and outcomes across included studies?
answers:
Malignancy rate in 30 case series with 96,578 subjects with
SLE was 3.4% (95% CI, 0.0260-0.0442).

We performed a subgroup analysis according to different
SLE onset ages, defining childhood-onset subjects at 18 years or
younger comparedwith those older than 18 years at SLE onset.Mean
follow-up time (<=5 vs. >5 years) subgroups were also compared.

question: Was between-study variation (heterogeneity) minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers:
Malignancy rate in 30 case series with 96,578 subjects with
SLE was 3.4% (95% CI, 0.0260-0.0442). There was a significant
heterogeneity (I2 = 97.6%) showing the consistency of clinical and
methodological aspects between the studies included in the metaanalysis
(P < 0.0001) (Fig. 2).
Malignancy rates in 25 adult and 5 childhood-onset SLE series
were 4.2% (95% CI, 0.0318-0.0531) and 0.5% (95% CI,
0.0003-0.0154), respectively. There was a significant difference
comparing the groups according to SLE onset age, as the 95%
CIs did not overlap (Fig. 2).Also, statistical heterogeneity was significant:
adult-onset SLE I2 = 98%, P < 0.0001, and childhoodonset
SLE, I2 = 62.6%, P = 0.03.
Reported follow-up was grouped as mean follow-up time of
less than or equal to 5 years and more than 5 years. Malignancy
rate from 11 studieswith a total of 34,462 SLE subjects withmean
follow-up of less than or equal to 5 years was 2.8% (95% CI,
0.013-0.047), whereas the rate in 16 studies with a total of
30,724 SLE subjects,withmean follow-up timemore than 5 years,
was 3.6% (95% CI, 0.0226-0.0531). Follow-up time was not reported
in 2 of the reported studies, comprising 19,475 SLE subjects
(Table 1). There was no significant difference between
these groups as the CIs overlap. Also, I2 heterogeneity was significant
in those with less than 5 years' and more than 5 years'
mean follow-up time, I2 = 91%, P < 0.0001, and I2 = 98.3%,
P < 0.0001, respectively

question: Were the findings robust, e.g. as demonstrated through funnel plot or sensitivity analyses?
answers: nan

question: Were biases in primary studies minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers: Overall, included studies were rated with low risk of bias for the following domains: selection of exposed and nonexposed cohorts from the same population, confidence in the assessment of exposure (SLE), outcome (malignancy), and follow-up time.

question: Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers: Our study limitations were the wide variation of studies compiled and limited reports about age range stratification in childhood-onset and adult-onset SLE, malignancy site, and organ involvement comparisons, as well as comorbidity additional risk factors. There was also a wide methodological variation with only 1 inception cohort24 and a few healthy population-controlled studies.9,25,43 Most of included population studies were reports of the National Cancer Registry matched to hospital discharge diagnoses as case-finding strategy, but smaller SLE series, from rheumatology practices, reporting damage index (SDI) were also pooled in the meta-analysis. The proportion of cases could be also a source of bias, but the search strategy and study selection allowed inclusion of all the published pediatric series with longterm follow-up, in order to power the analysis.
